Previous work has attempted to solve the dataset bottleneck by using simulated pairs, which are artificially created to address the lack of real data.
Simulated pairs are formed by starting with a clean image of a scene and artificially adding weather degradations to simulate various conditions.
Simulating the diverse weather conditions one can encounter is a difficult task, and existing simulators for rain are difficult to generalize.
Simulated pairs have been the most common approach, despite researchers encountering generalization errors.
Pseudo-real pairs are formed without the use of simulators and involve clean and degraded images without artificial additions.
One way to obtain pseudo-real pairs is to use a video-based deraining method to remove rain from images.
The use of simulators can be improved by employing a video-based deraining method to remove rain from a scene.
The video-based deraining method involves ground-truthing with limited performance in certain rain effects.
GT-RAIN is a recent method that focuses on introducing time multiplexed pairs for better results.
Time multiplexed pairs are obtained by grabbing frames with and without rain from input video sequences.
Using human annotation for time multiplexed pairing is challenging due to scalability issues.
Video sequences do not meet the criteria for a magic moment.
A further limit to scalability is that human observers must be highly trained to control for factors such as illumination shifts, weather API errors, and dynamic objects in videos.
In this paper, the authors formulate light transport techniques to help decide if frames should be included in the training data.
Contributions of the work involve formulating how time multiplexed pairs should be selected using light transport techniques.
The only previous approach is human annotated and limited to rain.
Automation scales the dataset, enabling the authors to obtain a dataset of 188K image pairs.
The dataset includes diverse rain and snow of different shapes, sizes, and strengths, resulting in a 1.5 dB improvement in performance across various state of the art baselines.

The first metric fgrad is a normalized measurement of the difference between the magnitudes of the gradients of two components (cid:101)c and (cid:101)d. This metric is calculated using Sobel filters.

The second metric ffft is a normalized measurement of the difference between the magnitudes of the low-pass filtered reconstructions of two components (cid:101)c and ¯d. The function flp is used to calculate the log scale magnitude spectrum of the low-pass filtered reconstructions.
